data.title: AMD Is Becoming The AI Market's Most Obvious Second Winner
data.description:
The Market Is Still Underestimating AMD’s Position

Investors have spent many years viewing Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) as a cyclical challenger as opposed to one of the most compelling companies in the world for the AI infrastructure era. That old way of viewing things is clearly no longer valid. AMD is now far more than just a CPU share-gain story. It has EPYC CPUs, Instinct accelerators, ROCm software, and the list goes on. This places the company much closer to the center of AI infrastructure spending than the market used to assume.

The most critical point in the system is not only who has the fastest single chip, but who can help customers deploy enough compute at scale. This clearly shows why AMD’s opportunity is expanding. In its most recent reported quarter, AMD said Q4 2025 revenue rose 36% year over year to $10.2 billion, while full-year 2025 revenue rose 32% to $33.7 billion. Data Center revenue was a record $4.3 billion in Q4, up 22% year over year, while full-year Data Center revenue reached a record $14.3 billion, up 39%.

AI Demand Is Moving Toward AMD’s Strengths

The most important part of the bullish case is that AI demand is not static. The market largely still likes to frame AI chips as a one-company story. This is a far underestimation of how large the AI buildout is becoming, and how much customers want a serious contender to Nvidia (NVDA).

AMD correctly described its AI roadmap as an open ecosystem strategy. The company revealed important plans like the Instinct MI350 series, continued ROCm development, and rack-scale designs. It also previewed Helios, a rack-scale reference design based on future MI400 GPUs, EPYC “Venice” CPUs, and Pensando networking. This is incredibly important as AI infrastructure is becoming more and more a full-stack systems problem rather than a single-chip purchasing decision.

AMD Is Building A Real Alternative Stack

AMD’s value proposition is not to hope that customers dislike Nvidia pricing. It is a real attempt to give hyperscalers and enterprises a plethora of other options. This could be a major selling point because the AI infrastructure market is now so large that even a small transfer of market share can make a significant impact on the bottom line.

The great thing about AMD is that it already has hands-on knowledge and experience in the parts of the data center that surround the accelerator. EPYC is highly relevant because CPUs remain incredibly vital for AI servers, cloud workloads, and general compute. Pensando is important because networking and data movement are no longer secondary. ROCm matters even more because its adoption will translate to far greater demand being funneled toward AMD hardware. Once you expand the lens, the AMD thesis starts to make far more sense.

The Financial Momentum Is Already Visible

AMD’s Q4 results are a huge indication of the market's positive shift in sentiment towards AMD. Client and Gaming segment revenue rose 37% year over year to $3.9 billion, while Client revenue alone hit a record $3.1 billion. Full-year Client and Gaming revenue rose a whopping 51% to a record $14.6 billion.

All of this is great news because AMD is capitalizing on urgent demand the most directly while also retaining exposure to more ordinary compute cycles. The company also predicts a Q1 2026 revenue to approximately $9.8 billion at the midpoint. This roughly translates to a 32% year-over-year growth. That is staggering numbers for any enterprise already operating at AMD’s scale.

The Real Bull Case Is Supply, Not Hype

The most mind-boggling valuations in AI usually belong to companies that investors believe control the scarce layer. Nvidia may be the undisputed king, but AMD could clearly deserve a richer valuation if it proves that the next wave of AI supply will not be owned by one vendor. The truly scarce resources are advanced packaging, HBM (high bandwidth memory), rack-level power delivery, networking, and software maturity. AMD sits a great deal closer to those constraints than the market appreciated five years ago.

This reasoning becomes even more undeniable when looking at AMD’s rack-scale plans. AMD says its open rack infrastructure can support up to 128 GPUs around MI350-series accelerators, EPYC CPUs, and Pensando networking. It also previewed Helios as the next major step for demanding AI workloads. This is incredibly important because the AI race is stubbornly entrenched in the physical world. The winner is not merely the company with the best demo. The winner is the company that can bottleneck power into immediate revenue.

Nvidia’s Lead Is Real, But AMD Does Not Need To Win Everything

The biggest mistake when analyzing AMD is assuming that the company must beat Nvidia outright to generate spectacular returns. That is the rather uninspired argument investors often use when they dismiss AMD. In reality, AMD only needs to become the most credible alternative in a market where customers are paying exorbitant amounts for compute and waiting for capacity.

This is hugely important because AI infrastructure demand is large enough to allow for relatively consistent multi-winner growth. AMD can be a smart diversified bet for investors who want exposure to AI infrastructure without paying the most extreme Nvidia premium. In just a few years time, the market may find out that the next wave of supply is not only about the most visible face of AI chips, but rather about the companies expanding capacity across CPUs, GPUs, and networking.

Risks Remain Meaningful

The bull case is powerful, but it should not be held at just the level of excitement. AMD still faces an incredibly lumpy growth trajectory. AI accelerator revenue can depend on large customer orders, product timing, software readiness, and supply availability. Gross margins can also experience some periods of relative volatility if AMD must price aggressively to win share or if newer products ramp less smoothly than expected.

There is also perhaps short-term inflated investor expectation. AMD is already priced in to some degree as an AI winner, and that creates risk if MI350 or MI400 adoption comes in slower than expected. Nvidia remains deeply entrenched, ROCm still has to continue to collaborate with developers and customers, and the wider and more highly competitive AI market can be eroded by custom silicon from hyperscalers. That said, none of these risks destroy the core thesis. They simply mean the path will be incredibly jagged.

The Valuation Could Still Make Sense

AMD may not look cheap on traditional semiconductor metrics, but the market usually most heavily reward companies when they are placed at the most important part of a major paradigm shift. AI compute is not a passing and non-sticky trend. It is preparing for a deep structural explosion across cloud, enterprise software, agents, consumer devices, and scientific workloads. The rise of agentic tooling should further increase demand for inference capacity, which coincides well with AMD’s accelerator and CPU roadmap.

The more bewildering features of the current landscape is that investors still sometimes treat AMD like a normal chip cycle stock. That is quickly becoming outdated. If AMD continues to execute, value should rapidly move toward companies most directly in contact with AI infrastructure expansion. AMD is one of the most obvious beneficiaries of this trend because it is doing the most to capitalize on a market where customers want more supply, more choice, and more leverage against incumbent pricing.

Final Takeaway

AMD is not merely chasing Nvidia. It is building a highly attractive alternative AI platform. The company’s record Data Center revenue, stronger Client business, and expanding AI roadmap further establishes that this is not a speculative turnaround story. It is a scaling AI infrastructure story with real revenue behind it.

AMD is cementing itself as the most credible second force in AI compute, and that could be more important than investors currently appreciate. For long-term investors, AMD looks like an incredibly attractive entry point into a company that is expanding its reach to the next era of computing.

data.title: Kazia Therapeutics: Diverse Cancer Pipeline Still Needs A Major Catalyst
data.description:
Kazia Therapeutics Limited (KZIA) is a clinical-stage oncology company that develops several programs. Their lead asset is Paxalisib, in Phase 2/3 in glioblastoma (GBM), with results in GBM showing meaningful concurrent-control survival data. Paxalisib is also studied in multiple central nervous system (CNS) related indications that are studied in Phase 2, such as diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma (DIPG), atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor (AT/RT), brain metastases, and central nervous system lymphoma. Paxalisib is a candidate for triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). Other programs include EVT801 for advanced solid tumors, NDL2 as a PD-L1 protein degrader, and a new platform called SETDB1. Despite this diverse pipeline, I ultimately think KZIA lacks concrete near-term catalysts that could rerate the stock higher, which is why I’m neutral for now.

Paxalisib: Overlooked Oncology

Kazia Therapeutics Limited is a clinical-stage biotechnology company that develops drugs for the treatment of cancers of the central nervous system (CNS) and solid tumors. KZIA traces its roots to Novogen Limited, which was founded back in 1994. It later changed its strategy and its name to Kazia in 2016. The company is currently headquartered in Sydney, Australia. Interestingly, I noticed this small biotech has a relatively diverse oncology pipeline, yet it’s mostly undercovered despite targeting sizable TAMs for cancer treatments. The stock has also been performing well since its 2025 lows, so I thought it was worthwhile looking into this name.

As a quick overview, KZIA’s leading asset is Paxalisib, which Genentech, a member of the Roche Group (RHHBY), initially developed. Eventually, KZIA entered into a worldwide exclusive agreement with Genentech to develop and commercialize the drug. Under the agreement, Novogen/Kazia paid Genentech $5 million, with additional payments tied to milestones and royalties.

With that in mind, Paxalisib is a dual PI3K and mTOR inhibitor, which means that it targets the growth-and-survival control circuit inside the cell. When this pathway works normally, it helps healthy cells respond to growth signals, but in cancer, the pathway becomes overactive. This allows tumor cells to keep growing, avoid cell death, and resist therapy. Thus, Paxalisib is designed to block PI3K/mTOR, potentially interrupting this pathway, with a stronger effect than if it were a single-point inhibitor. Paxalisib is also an oral drug, capable of reaching the brain, crossing the blood-brain barrier (BBB), which is useful since delivering treatments into the brain can be tricky due to the BBB.

Moreover, Paxalisib is studied for glioblastoma (GBM), a common primary brain cancer, and for IDH-mutant glioma. Also, the candidate is tested for triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). For GBM, the trial GBM AGILE is in Phase 2/3. This program is made possible with the collaboration of Weill Cornell Medicine, Global Coalition for Adaptive Research, and the University of Sydney. And it’s worth mentioning that KZIA’s GBM AGILE trial uses accumulating data to keep updating the probability that the treatment is helping patients.

So far, this trial has produced positive results for newly diagnosed unmethylated glioblastoma (uGBM). They compared Paxalisib’s data in a) a primary analysis with trial patients treated with the standard of care. This primary analysis wasn’t limited to patients enrolled during the same time as the paxalisib arm (cumulative standard of care). They also compared it against b) a secondary analysis with patients treated with standard of care administered during the same period as paxalisib patients (concurrent standard of care). And KZIA’s corporate overview mentioned they’re having discussions with the FDA regarding a regulatory pathway in GBM using these datasets.

After all, Paxalisib’s primary analysis showed a median overall survival (mOS) of 14.77 months, and the cumulative standard of care a mOS of 13.84 months. This was only a small difference of around 0.9 months. However, the secondary analysis had a mOS of 15.54 months, and concurrent standard of care reported a mOS of 11.89 months. This means Paxalisib reported a 3.8-month mOS improvement, which is much more promising. As such, KZIA believes this secondary analysis may be a cleaner real-time comparison. Plus, this secondary review might be more clinically meaningful considering that these patients had poor outcomes and limited treatment options.

Diverse Oncology Assets

Aside from that, KZIA also has a Phase 2 for Paxalisib targeting other central nervous system (CNS) indications. Concretely, they’re looking into diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma (DIPG) and atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor (AT/RT), which are childhood brain cancers. The DIPG program is developed in partnership with Pacific Pediatric Neuro-Oncology Consortium, St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital, and the Australian and New Zealand Children’s Hematology/Oncology Group (ANZCHOG). The AT/RT research has the collaboration of the Pacific Pediatric Neuro-Oncology Consortium and Johns Hopkins Medicine.

Additionally, Paxalisib has a Phase 2 trial for brain metastases in partnership with the National Cancer Institute, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, and Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center. Another Phase 2 study is for CNS lymphoma, which is a type of non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma, in collaboration with the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute.

For TNBC, the trial ABC-PAX is in Phase 1b. It's studying Paxalisib in combination with a) Keytruda, which is an immune checkpoint inhibitor to boost the immune attack, potentially benefiting traditional chemotherapy. And, they’re also testing Paxalisib with b) Lynparza, which is a PARP inhibitor designed to exploit the tumor cell´s weakness in repairing DNA damage. And KZIA is progressing this program in collaboration with QIMR Berghofer. The trial treated 24 patients with metastatic TNBC, and it reported a significant reduction of tumors by 86%.

Similarly, KZIA completed a Phase 1 for EVT801, which is designed to interfere with biological processes that help tumors form lymphatic routes for metastasis. This candidate is tested for advanced solid tumors in patients with highly treatment-resistant cancer. The program is developed in partnership with Institut Universitaire du Cancer de Toulouse and the Center Léon Bérard.

Lastly, in the preclinical stage, KZIA is developing a PD-L1 Protein Degrader Platform, with its main candidate, NDL2. PD-L1 is a protein that some cancer cells use to escape immune attack. Checkpoint inhibitors like Keytruda usually work by blocking the PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, allowing T cells to attack the tumor. KZIA’s approach is different. NDL2 is designed to break down PD-L1 inside the cancer cell. NDL2 is being developed to treat solid tumors that are resistant to immunotherapy in collaboration with QIMR Berghofer.

As a final note on their pipeline, KZIA recently added a new preclinical oncology platform, SETDB1. This is intended for cancers that evade the immune system, aiming to reactivate suppressed immune signaling pathways. The platform was licensed from QIMR Berghofer, with the candidate MSETC. The deal includes an upfront payment of $1.39 million and a revenue-sharing structure, without milestone obligations. This SETDB1 program has a defined path for IND-enabling studies, and it's expected to advance in parallel with its PD-L1 degrader platform. And KZIA estimated the cost to advance both programs toward IND submission to be around $6 million over 18 months.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, KZIA currently trades at a $136.6 million market cap. It’s also a thinly traded microcap with only 210.3 thousand shares in average volume, which at the current $12.00 PPS, implies a shallow trading liquidity of $2.5 million. This makes the stock inherently much riskier, so please bear that in mind.

In any event, its latest report (in Australian Dollars) as of December 2025 shows its balance sheet holds A$69.5 million (~$49.7 million) in cash against only A$98.8 thousand (~$70.7 thousand) in borrowings (aside from other regular operating liabilities). However, as you might expect, they still don’t have any meaningful revenue. In fact, using their cash flows from operations as a proxy, I estimate they burned through A$9.5 million (~$6.8 million) during 2H2025. That would imply a cash runway of 3.7 years going forward, and management itself projects they have enough financing into 2029.

Besides, since we can’t use other valuation metrics, I will point out that they currently have an A$46.5 million (~$33.3 million) book value. This implies a slightly expensive P/B of 4.1, which is a definite premium over its sector’s median P/B of 2.7. But the reality is that their pipeline is so diverse that they’re technically targeting several multibillion-dollar TAMs. In that sense, I do believe KZIA deserves some optionality premium. After all, it’s possible that Big Pharma may like a particular asset and could consider KZIA as a relatively cheap acquisition to incorporate its IP. Naturally, this is inherently speculative at this point, and currently, management seems to be focusing on looking for partnerships to fund its R&D rather than potential buyers.

Furthermore, I think the clearest value driver right now is probably Paxalisib for GBM. And the timeline here is probably going to take several years unless KZIA convinces the FDA of some kind of expedited pathway. Unfortunately, back in 2024, the FDA stated KZIA’s GBM-AGILE wouldn’t support an accelerated approval pathway. Plus, I imagine that if KZIA actually starts a pivotal GBM Phase 3 trial, its cash burn would probably increase, decreasing its runway by extension. And, aside from Paxalisib, the rest of KZIA’s pipeline seems to be in very early stages or even preclinical in some cases.

Conclusion: Neutral But Worth Watching

Overall, KZIA has a promising pipeline, and I think Paxalisib is easily its main value driver for now. In theory, KZIA could announce at some point a major partnership or M&A transaction with larger biopharmaceuticals. After all, Paxalisib did show some gains in mOS, which can be invaluable for those patients with few options left. So, major partnerships or M&A deals could quickly unlock significant shareholder value. Unfortunately, aside from those types of highly speculative catalysts, I don’t see any near-term events that could help rerate the stock higher. Hence, I believe KZIA is likely going to trade sideways for a while, which is why I’m neutral despite its otherwise interesting oncology pipeline.

data.title: Prepare For A Pullback Because Q2 Is Going To Be Ugly
data.description:
The relentless stock market rally that began in early April was struck with a one-two punch yesterday. The first was a surge in oil prices overnight with WTI crude surpassing $100 again, while Brent rose above $111, as negotiations between Iran and the Trump administration remain in a stalemate. The second was a blind side hook from the technology sector when a report surfaced that OpenAI fell short of its own goals for new users and revenues. That sent shockwaves through those names banking on future infrastructure sales to the AI giant, led by Oracle and CoreWeave. Infrastructure spending on AI is the driving force behind this year’s stock market resilience, as well as a significant percentage of our tepid overall rate of economic growth today.

The next test will be today’s earnings from heavyweights Alphabet, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta Platforms. All four have rebounded sharply from their correction lows with Amazon and Alphabet cresting new all-time highs. Apple will report tomorrow. These names collectively account for a quarter of the S&P 500’s total value, so their results will likely determine whether the uptrend continues or stalls. My concern since last fall has been that the rate of earnings and revenue growth for these companies, while still stellar, is slowing. This is why they have collectively flatlined in terms of performance over the past six months, and I don’t think upcoming reports will change that. The Magnificent 7 (MAGS) are still down less than 1% year-to-date.

Meanwhile, an increasing number of companies are mentioning the war in Iran as a concern on earnings conference calls. It remains to be seen whether this will lead to lower estimates for the overall S&P 500, but with the headwinds mostly in front of us and having little impact on first quarter results, I think we will see numbers inch lower. Thankfully, the market has the buffer of record profit margins and strong earnings growth, so the economic headwinds that hit us in the second quarter are likely to make great fundamentals still pretty good rather than negative. Still, I think the deterioration will stall the rally in the coming months, leading to another pullback.

The high-frequency economic data during the remainder of the second quarter is likely to get very ugly, as the K-shaped recovery becomes even more extreme. The national average for gas prices just hit a new high since the war began at $4.22, and with WTI crude up another $3 overnight to $103.

Gas prices have been the only visible impact on our economy from the war in Iran, but business surveys from NFIB, the Institute for Supply Management (ISM), and S&P Global show that input costs for manufacturers and services companies are soaring at rates not seen since the last inflation spike in 2022. These producer prices are going to start to feed into goods and services prices for consumers over the coming weeks and months. Airline ticket prices are already absurd. The market has looked past this inflationary spiral as temporary, but the adverse impacts will linger and be front and center when earnings season ends.

Two weeks ago, I noted the seemingly insatiable appetite for stocks by Commodity Trading Advisors (CTAs), which are professional asset managers that employ a systematic trend following discipline. In other words, they chase momentum in both directions using futures and options on all asset classes. Over the past month, they have been chasing this stock market rally to the tune of an estimated $53 billion, according to Goldman Sachs, moving for the short side at the correction low to what is now extremely long as the indexes crest at all-time highs. With momentum starting to wane, and some very challenging economic data ahead, I suspect these buyers to be sellers as the market pulls back, accentuating any decline.

At quarter’s end, we are likely to see other asset allocators rebalance out of equities and into bonds to realign portfolios with benchmarks. As the excitement of the first-quarter earnings season passes and investors are forced to refocus on inflation and slower rates of economic growth, I see a 3-5% pullback in the S&P 500 setting the stage for the next advance once we move through the trials of the second quarter. A mere 5% pullback would let the market test its 50-day moving average and resolve the overbought levels we see today.

data.title: IDEF: Defense Selloff Creates A Compelling Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Aerospace & defense ETFs plunged into a correction territory after years of staggering returns. The selloff presents an attractive buying opportunity as secular uptrend is expected to accelerate in the quarters and years ahead. The recently launched iShares Defense Industrials Active ETF (IDEF) could be a solid investment vehicle for dip buyers and long term investors.

IDEF seeks to maximize returns by holding positions in aerospace & defense and related-companies with the potential to benefit from higher spending. It outperformed popular aerospace & defense ETFs since its inception due to multiple factors, including global approach, diversification and active portfolio management. The fund's expense ratio is in-line with peers while liquidity is growing sharply. I initiate coverage of IDEF with a buy rating.

Defense Industry’s Secular Growth Seems Strong

iShares Defense Industrials Active ETF, iShares US Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA), Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD) and Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF (PPA) plunged sharpy after hitting record levels. The selloff is supported by the potential US-Iran deal. The investors profit-taking added to a price decline.

The selloff is likely to be temporary, given the rising global military spending, increased geo-political conflicts and race to equip armies with the latest defense technologies. According to the latest report from Stockholm International Peace Research Institute, military spending increased to $2.9 trillion in 2025 from $2.7 trillion in 2024. This marks 11th consecutive year of growth and represents 2.9% year-over-year boost. The spending was equivalent to 2.5% of the global GDP, the highest level since 2009. Excluding the US, the global military spending increased by more than 9% in 2025.

Furthermore, the spending is expected to surge in 2026 and beyond. The US military spending could cross a $1 trillion level in 2026. Moreover, given the latest budget proposal, US military spending in 2027 could rise to a $1.5 trillion level for the first time in history. As the US is the world’s largest military spender, a 42% boost in its military budget can significantly spark demand for defense products.

On the other hand, NATO members are expected to lift military spending in 2026 following 2025’s fastest annual increase since 1953. Rising tensions between East Asian countries continue to accelerate their military spending. China spent $336 billion in 2025, up more than 7% year-over-year. Taiwan’s spending grew 14% to $18 billion in 2025 due to its tensions with China. Beijing seeks to invade Taiwan amid its territorial claims. Taiwan is expected to bolster it military budget above $30 billion in 2026. Japan spent $62 billion in 2025, equivalent to 1.5% of its GDP. The country seeks to intensify its spending to 1.9% of its GDP in 2026.

Iran’s attacks on the Middle Eastern energy infrastructure has boosted their race to acquire latest defense technologies to achieve a dominating position in the region. For example, during the first quarter of 2026, out of the total global approvals of $45 billion in foreign military sales, the Gulf region is expected to receive $36.6 billion of defense equipment. The Saudi Ministry of Defense plans to spend nearly $64 billion on its defense.

Overall, given a record-breaking defense spending and rising conflicts across the world, the secular defense growth trend is expected to last in 2026 and ahead.

IDEF's Portfolio Diversification Contributes to Market-Beating Returns

As the aerospace & defense industry’s selloff seems temporary, selecting the right investment vehicle is crucial to capitalize on gains. iShares Defense Industrials Active ETF could be a worth considering investment vehicle, as the fund demonstrated strong performance since its launch in 2025. IDEF’s share price upside outshined its peers, including iShares US Aerospace & Defense ETF, Global X Defense Tech ETF and Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF.

IDEF’s outperformance is attributed to its strategy of holding stakes in companies with the potential to benefit the most from increased defense spending. An active portfolio management contributes in maximizing returns while limiting a risk factor. IDEF does not track any specific index and uses a global approach in its stock selection process, seeking to hold stakes in the aerospace & defense and related-companies. It intends to maintain at least 50% of its assets in aerospace & defense industry. As discussed above, the global defense industry is likely to accelerate its growth.

Of the IDEF’s top 10 holdings, RTX Corporation (RTX), Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT), General Dynamics Corporation (GD), Northrop Grumman Corporation (NOC), Rheinmetall AG (RNMBF) and Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB), and the rest of portfolio includes a long list of defense companies from across the world.

In addition, IDEF’s exposure to aerospace companies has also been contributing to its share price upside. By early 2026, the global passenger jet backlog jumped to a new all-time high of 17,000 aircrafts. In the first quarter, The Boeing Company's (BA) commercial aircrafts achieved a record backlog of over 6,100 airplanes. Rolls-Royce Holdings plc’s (RYCEY) annual profit was up 40% in 2025. Both Boeing and Rolls-Royce are among IDEF's top 10 holdings.

Similarly, companies offering products and equipments to an aerospace industry highlights a robust growth. For example, Woodward, Inc. (WWD), which offers fuel pumps, actuators, metering units, deck controls and several other products for aircrafts, is expected to deliver nearly 30% earnings growth in 2026. Its shares are up nearly 100% in the last twelve months. Carpenter Technology Corporation (CRS), which provides alloy solutions and other specialized products for aerospace and defense industry, has seen a share price rally of 112% in the last twelve months. The company expects operating income growth in the range of 30% to 33% in 2026. Woodward, Carpenter Technology and many other specialized products providers are also within IDEF’s portfolio.

Furthermore, Palantir Technologies, Inc. (PLTR), one of IDEF’s top holdings, provides software platforms for the intelligence community. The fund’s top 10 holdings also include Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, Ltd. (MHVYF).

On the whole, IDEF’s portfolio diversification and exposure to global markets positions it to offer market-beating returns. On the other hand, iShares US Aerospace & Defense ETF offers exposure to the US market with portfolio of 48 stocks. Global X Defense Tech ETF concentrates only on defense stocks. While the Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF offers exposure to 63 stocks, the fund does not consider stocks from global markets and related-industries.

IDEF Characteristics

iShares Defense Industrials Active ETF’s portfolio comprises 110 stocks, with the top 10 holdings accounting for 42% of its entire weight. It is an actively managed ETF. Its portfolio managers can exit or decrease positions in the low performing stocks while replacing them with better options. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.55% is in-line with its peers. However, IDEF’s portfolio turnover could be higher due to an active management strategy.

Moreover, a significant boost in its liquidity in a short time demonstrates investors growing interest. IDEF’s assets under management stand around $3.50 billion while average shares trading volume hover around 1.9 million. The fund can also pay dividends because the majority of aerospace & defense companies offer healthy dividends to shareholders. Furthermore, at present, the fund trades around $33 per share. A low per share price can boost investors potential to buy shares in a large quantity. On the other hand, ITA SHLD and PPA trades around $215, $67 and $166 per share.

Risk Factors to Consider

iShares Defense Industrials Active ETF carries a higher risk factor because of its exposure to international markets and multiple industries. Its exposure to international markets can increase currency, regulation and volatility risks. As defense & aerospace contracts are often long-term, currency fluctuations can negatively impact the corporate profitability. The sanctions and increased regulations can reduce defense & aerospace demand. Global economic and monetary conditions can also create challenges for the industry.

In Conclusion

As the aerospace and defense industry’s secular growth trend is expected to extend into the quarters and years ahead, dip buying seems to be a prudent strategy. iShares Defense Industrials Active ETF has the potential to make the most out of uptrend, given the fund's strategy of holding positions in the fast-growing and fundamentally sound companies. Its expense ratio is in-line with peers while liquidity is growing. IDEF’s active management can also help in limiting a downside risk while capitalizing on growth opportunities.

data.title: APA Corporation: No Hedges, No Limits--Perfectly Positioned To Benefit From Eleveted Oil Prices
data.description:
I have covered APA Corporation (APA) before, where I outlined the company's background in detail and explained why I considered it an attractive buy. Since then the stock has moved more than 50%, in line with its peers, due to the Iran war and the Strait of Hormuz closure; however, APA will be one of the most benefited companies as it does not have oil hedges and will fully benefi from the price surge. This increase in free cash flow will directly flow to shareholders as the company has a 60% return to shareholders policy and will allow the company to further reduce debt, being one of the oil companies with the lowest debt levels.

Background

APA Corporation is an oil and gas company with a diversified production profile in three regions and also three exploration areas at different stages.

The company exited 2025 producing roughly 460,000 boe per day, with about 53% oil, 30% gas and the rest NGLs.

The Permian is the main production basin for the company with 281,000 boe per day. APA operates across both the Midland and Delaware basins. In this basin the company is not focused on growth, but on maximizing free cash flow as it has been able to hold production flat while running fewer rigs and spending less capital. Drilling and completion costs are down around 30% since 2024, which significantly improves returns.

The second producing region is Egypt which is a significant asset that the market tends to overlook. Especially as updated terms in Q1 2025 have improved gas realizations that allocate higher fixed price on incremental volumes above predetermined levels. Moreover, last month there was a significant gas discovery in the Western Desert that pose well for future growth in the region.

This ensures Egypt will increase its contribution from both production and margin perspective. Here, the company does not benefit from elevated gas prices as they are paid a fixed amount; however, oil prices are linked to brent.

Finally, the third production region is the North Sea that continues to decline. It still generates some cash but will continue to go down as they don’t invest to maintain production due to elevated taxes.

Regarding exploration, the main asset is Suriname:

The GranMorgu project continues to move forward with drilling in Q4 2026 and first oil targeted around 2028. Despite inflation, costs continue to be in line with estimates. The project will substantially increase production and cash flow as it is expected to generate 220,000 b/d of oil with an attractive development cost of $14 per barrel and OPEX below $20 per barrel.

Besides its assets, APA has a Differential Gas Trading Portfolio, combining Permian transport with exposure to global LNG pricing. The company was guiding for around $650 million of pre tax cash flow in 2026 and was expecting a reduction in the following years.

However, with disruptions in Qatar and tighter LNG markets, spreads have widened, which could favor its trading division not only this year but also in the future.

Regarding capital allocation, APA has a policy to return at least 60% free cash flow to shareholders through dividends and buybacks and use the rest to reduce debt until reaching its $3 billion long term debt target. Management was expecting to reach that target in about 3 years; however, given the current surge in prices and lack of hedges, I believe the company could achieve it at the beginning of 2027.

Even before Suriname, the current asset base generates enough cash to provide attractive returns and improve the balance sheet. Once Suriname comes online, the free cash flow should improve even more.

Financial Position and Valuation

APA Corp has a comfortable financial position with $516 million in cash and around $4.5 billion of debt as of Q4 2025. Net debt is below $4.0 billion with one of the lowest leverages in the sector at 0.7x EBITDAX.

APA reported great Q4 results with EPS of $0.91 beating expectations and also provided 2026 guidance:

Full year production is expected to be around 436,000 boe per day, below 2025 levels, mainly due to North Sea declines.

The company also expects lower CapEx at around $2.1 billion, which will further improve free cash flow generation. Moreover, they are ahead on cost saving with already above $350 million which should support margins into 2026.

The company also indicates its sensitivity to oil prices, with a cash flow sensitivity of $265 million for every $5 change in prices.

Assuming an $80 WTI price and higher gas differentials, I estimate the company could achieve close to $2 billion in free cash flow in 2026. To be on the conservative side and assuming $1.8 billion free cash flow, the company is trading at a 13.5% FCF yield. I believe this FCF yield is too elevated for an oil company with very limited debt. Assuming a 10% FCF yield it provides an $18 billion market capitalization, or a fair value of $51.5 per share, representing more than a 35% upside.

Moreover, with that free cash flow, debt could be reduced about $700 million, almost reaching the company’s long term target of $3 billion in net debt.

I believe a $80 WTI price is achievable in 2026 because in the event of a prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz, around 13 million barrels per day are offline. This causes inventories to draw down rapidly and even if there is a resolution, restoring supply will take time. Moreover, inventories will need to be replenished, supporting oil prices for longer.

Despite this, Q1 results will not fully grasp the benefits from higher oil prices and I expect them to be the lowest of the year.

I expect EPS of around $1.25, slightly above analysts' expectations:

Risks

The main risk remains commodity price volatility. A faster than expected resolution in Iran or weaker demand could pressure oil and gas prices.

Another risks given the US production is if White House decides to halt oil exports. I don’t expect that to happen; however, if it does, WTI price will be much lower than brent price, affecting US realized prices.

There is also execution risk, particularly in Suriname. Large offshore projects always carry some uncertainty even if everything is currently on track.

Finally, the gas trading business depends on spreads that could normalize over time.

Conclusion

APA Corporation is already generating strong cash flow, even before the Suriname production increase.

Since the company does not have oil hedges, it will fully benefit from increased oil prices, which will deliver substantial shareholder returns and debt reduction.

Moreover, its gas differentials trading division will also benefit from the disruption of Qatar LNG, providing tailwinds for several years.

Taking all that into consideration, I believe the stock is undervalued and my fair value is $51.5 per share.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Trump Accounts Hit Robinhood Expenses
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

The following is an abridged transcript:

Robinhood Markets (HOOD) is tumbling premarket after Q1 earnings and revenue both missed estimates, and the company lifted its expense outlook due to the impact of Trump Accounts.

GAAP EPS came in at $0.38, just below the $0.39 consensus. Revenue of $1.07B trailed expectations of $1.14B. Average revenue per user fell to $157 from $191 in the prior quarter.

Transaction-based revenue declined to $623M from $776M in Q4. Crypto revenue dropped 47% Y/Y to $134M, while options revenue rose 8% to $250M and equities revenue jumped 46% to $82M.

Robinhood also raised its 2026 combined adjusted operating expense and share-based compensation guidance to $2.7B–$2.825B, above prior guidance and consensus.

“Our work for Trump accounts is contracted on a cost plus basis with a small margin, so we expect revenues to exceed costs,” the company said.

Investment manager Ross Gerber of Gerber Kawasaki said Robinhood only makes money “when you gamble and lose on.... stock options, crypto and betting. This is a gambling app, nothing more. They make money when you lose.”

President Donald Trump pressed Iran to “get smart soon” after reportedly telling aides to prepare for an extended blockade of the country’s key port infrastructure.

The decision to maintain the blockade is seen as a lower-risk alternative to resuming bombing or abandoning negotiations altogether, the Wall Street Journal reported.

Trump said Iran’s offer to reopen the Strait of Hormuz while postponing nuclear talks showed Tehran was not negotiating in good faith.

“Iran can't get their act together,” Trump posted overnight on Truth Social. “They don't know how to sign a nonnuclear deal. They better get smart soon!”

An extended blockade would likely keep energy markets on edge, with traders watching closely for any disruption tied to the Strait of Hormuz — a key chokepoint for global oil flows.

As Bloomberg columnist Javier Blas noted, one trader quipped that markets were expecting “TACO” — Trump Always Chickens Out — but are instead getting “NACHO” — Not A Chance Hormuz Opens.

Disney (DIS) has reportedly decided against spinning off ESPN and will keep the sports network within its portfolio, Business Insider reports.

The decision comes as Josh D’Amaro prepares to assume leadership from Bob Iger. Keeping ESPN in-house is seen as supporting Disney’s broader pivot to streaming, with the network continuing to be offered across cable and streaming platforms, including bundled packages with Hulu+ and Disney+.

ESPN was once a major profit engine for Disney but has faced subscriber declines amid cord-cutting. While Iger publicly described ESPN as a core asset, discussions had emerged over whether separating the network could unlock value.

Now Here’s What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Seagate Technology (STX) is surging after results and guidance topped estimates.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) exited Arm Holdings (ARM) with a $231M share sale.

JPMorgan’s Jamie Dimon is warning of a looming bond market crisis.

In premarket trading, stock index futures (SPY) (INDU) (US100:IND) are little changed as investors await the Fed rate decision and Chairman Jay Powell’s remarks, following the end of the DOJ probe and amid a Senate Banking Committee vote on Kevin Warsh’s nomination to be the next chair.

And for the corporate crowd, four of the Magnificent 7 report earnings after the bell.

On the economic calendar:

8:30 am – Durable Goods (March)

8:30 am – Housing Starts & Permits (February/March)

10:00 am – New Home Sales (February/March)

2:00 pm – FOMC Decision

data.title: Western Digital's Q3 Preview: Great News From An AI Industry Peer
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Western Digital Corp. (NYSE:WDC), one of two leading American technology companies specializing in data storage solutions for AI applications, is scheduled to report its Q3 FY26 earnings tomorrow after markets close.

WDC's storage business continues to be flooded with a tidal wave of demand from hyperscalers and enterprises trying to buy up as many hard disk drives or HDDs for their AI inference applications. Tomorrow's Q3 ER will detail just how much of a tailwind AI inference has been for WDC until now and for many quarters to come.

The good news is that WDC's other direct rival and data storage industry peer, Seagate (STX), just reported its own Q3 FY26 ER, which showed that HDD demand is so strong that Seagate's capacity is sold out through next year as well.

Strong results by WDC's rival yesterday further showcase how undervalued WDC looks before its Q3 earnings print tomorrow, which is why I initiate my coverage on WDC shares with a bullish view.

WDC Is Poised To Outperform In Tomorrow's Q3 ER - See Seagate

Over the past few months it has become increasingly apparent to me that the entire HDD industry's capacity to ship HDDs is severely constrained by robust demand supporting AI inference applications, and that demand shows no signs of stopping.

The ever-increasing requirements to make AI models more contextually aware continue to place unprecedented demand strain on memory and storage products that leading AI players like hyperscalers & frontier model companies use to support AI inference workloads.

The combined insight from the Q2 FY26 earnings calls of Seagate and Western Digital, America's 2 leading manufacturers of HDDs and storage products, 3 months ago led markets to a firm consensus: "Hyperscalers are soaking up all the high-capacity storage" because of "the AI infrastructure boom."

Nearline storage has been the product face of the HDD industry because these next-gen HDDs are often seen by hyperscalers as cost-effective due to high storage capacity while still packing in high performance. Seagate's HAMR-based Mozaic HDD and WDC's Ultrastar/Gold HDDs are nearline HDDs that are most sought after by hyperscalers, and both HDD storage companies keep getting capacity-constrained to supply hyperscalers with these HDDs.

In the Q2 FY26 call, WDC's management had announced that they were "pretty much sold out" in terms of the company's CY26 capacity to produce and ship HDDs for its hyperscaler customers. At the time, WDC's management even noted that they had already started to sign storage capacity agreements with hyperscalers for HDD capacity into CY27 as well.

Based on that outlook 3 months ago, WDC revealed the company would achieve ~$3.3B in Q3 FY26 revenues, implying 39.5% top line growth while gross margins would expand by an impressive 740 bp to ~47.5%. If WDC actually ends up delivering these numbers tomorrow, which I believe it easily will, the company will have already overachieved some of its FY26 targets announced at a prior Innovation Day event.

But after observing Seagate's blowout earnings performance, I believe WDC will most likely hike its guidance tomorrow, not only for the upcoming Q4 FY26 quarter but also for FY27, which starts in June.

Seagate's Q3 ER shows a strong double beat in their Q3, but something that management said really stood out to me, and I believe this has real positive implications for WDC too.

Yesterday, Seagate's management told investors that the company's capacity for producing and shipping nearline HDDs was "almost fully allocated through calendar 2027." To me, that revelation by Seagate's management was staggering because it represented a sharp escalation in forward demand Seagate was seeing.

Three months ago, Seagate announced that their nearline HDD capacity was "fully allocated through calendar year 2026" and that management expected to "begin accepting orders for the first half of calendar year 2027 in the coming months." This was a similar characterization of forward demand for CY27, just like I observed in WDC's management's forward-looking comments in that Q2 FY26 call to which I referred a few paragraphs earlier.

But for Seagate to have mostly allocated all of its nearline capacity for CY27, in just 3 months, speaks to the unprecedented, exponential demand tailwinds that the HDD industry continues to witness.

Therefore, in my view, this continues to be a situation where "all boats are rising," as previously noted by WDC's CFO, to characterize the generational tailwinds for the memory & storage industry.

This can also be seen in the steadily rising pace at which the HDD industry's two leading manufacturers are shipping out exabytes to customers, as noted below.

(The chart below includes Seagate's exabytes shipped data from their Q3 call yesterday.)

Given this updated backdrop and factoring in Seagate's strong Q3 print, I believe Western Digital's valuation multiples-33x FY26 EPS growing 83% or EV/forward sales of ~11x FY26 sales growing ~32-33%-are still cheap as the company gears up for tomorrow's Q3 print.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

The entire AI industry will be watching capex numbers and projected capex growth outlook when each of the top four hyperscalers report their respective earnings results today after markets close. This is the single largest catalyst for WDC, which will indicate just how much gas is left in the tank for the AI growth engine.

Additionally, here's what I'm watching for WDC tomorrow:

  • Q3 Expectations: $2.39 EPS (up 76%) on revenues worth $3.25B (up 41.6%)
  • Q4 Expectations: $2.75 EPS (up 66%) on revenues worth $3.47B (up 33%)
  • FY26E: $9.04 EPS (up 83.4%) on revenues worth $12.6B (up 32%)
  • Exabytes shipped: While WDC has shipped 215 exabytes so far, if I factor in the pace of exabyte shipment growth demonstrated by Seagate, I am personally anticipating WDC delivering exabyte shipments of ~225-230 EB.
  • I'll also be closely watching WDC management's characterization of capacity for CY27

Takeaway

Seagate's strong Q3 ER print from yesterday has certainly given Western Digital a further shot in the arm to outperform all expectations tomorrow. The HDD industry is facing unprecedented demand due to surging memory requirements from AI inference workloads, and I expect to see that Western Digital's Q3 print tomorrow.

I am bullish on Western Digital as it heads into Q3 ER tomorrow.

data.title: Amazon Quick Shows Why Agentic AI Needs The Desktop
data.description:
An interesting thing is happening in the world of agentic AI - it’s bringing more attention and focus back to client devices and even on-device compute. Initially, there was a prominent school of thought which posited that agentic AI would drive more centralization of computing resources. In a sense, the idea was that we would move back to a mainframe and terminal-like model, where client devices had little to no impact on the computing workloads. Instead, it looks like the exact opposite is occurring and the importance and relevance of client devices is continuing to grow.

Tools like Claude Cowork and OpenClaw, for example, are designed around computer use agents (CUAs), which leverage all the data stored on your own PC to perform a wide range of personalized, autonomous actions. Ironically, the latest example of the client-focused role for agentic AI is the new Amazon (AMZN) Quick desktop application, which the company just announced as part of a batch of news from their What’s Next with AWS event.

The Quick desktop app builds on the company’s previously announced mobile and web-based apps, but critically, adds the ability to tap into all the data, email, chat threads and more found on your local PC. Even more importantly - but surprisingly not mentioned at all by Amazon at the launch event - it leverages on-board computing resources to do so. According to Amazon, the Windows and macOS versions of Quick can take advantage of available local compute resources, including CPUs, GPUs and NPUs, for certain parts of the workflow.

Philosophically, this is a big deal because it validates the role of client devices in agentic AI. Practically, it makes sense because today’s AI PCs already contain meaningful local compute resources. Economically, it could matter even more because local pre-processing and orchestration can reduce unnecessary cloud calls.

Not only can this save costs, but it can also help enable more efficient usage of cloud resources. In an era where computing demands are growing and, in some cases, outpacing available capacity, this isn’t just prudent, it’s practical. Toss in future concerns regarding power grid capacity and one could argue this Hybrid AI computing model quickly becomes essential.

Strategically, it seems that Amazon is trying to make Quick less of an AWS front-end and more of a horizontal work layer - one that reaches across Microsoft, Google, Salesforce, ServiceNow, Slack, Teams and the local desktop itself.

As with other agentic tools that are starting to arrive on the scene, what Quick does is provide a means by which you can complete complex multi-part efforts (as well as simple tasks) through a simple chat-style interface. You make a request and it fulfills it by leveraging the data on your device. The requests can range from helping to prepare for a meeting with a new potential client and generating a pitch deck that ties in the unique characteristics and requirements of the company you’re meeting to sending a group email on a particular topic, and much more.

Behind the scenes, Quick is tapping into a Knowledge graph that it puts together by analyzing all the information you give it access to: your emails, documents, chats, SaaS tools, company resources, etc., and applying advanced analytics to that data. Quick leverages RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) techniques to combine that information with intelligence it can tap into via cloud-based LLMs. Thanks to today’s announcement with OpenAI - another big bit of news from the What’s Next with AWS event - that could mean OpenAI’s latest frontier models, as well as a huge range of different models from Anthropic, Amazon themselves and others.

The OpenAI news matters because it gives Amazon more than another model checkbox. It strengthens the back-end intelligence layer behind tools like Quick, while Bedrock provides the governance, identity, logging and deployment controls that enterprises will demand before letting agents operate across real business systems.

The challenge for Amazon is that there are a lot of competitive options being brought to market that are going to make it difficult to rise above the noise. Plus, while AWS has an enormous installed base of companies who rely on its cloud-based services, it’s not considered a serious player in the world of desktop applications. In fact, I think this could be part of the reason why the company chose to name the applications Amazon Quick and not AWS Quick. Convincing organizations that it should be the partner to provide the kind of agentic productivity intelligence you might expect from a Microsoft or Google, for example, is going to take some strong messaging.

To their credit, it seems as if Amazon has made the process of integrating data from common productivity suites and other desktop applications very simple. Plus, in larger organizations that have a lot of their most critical data resources stored within the confines of AWS, Amazon has a clear advantage in tapping into that information. And for the most sophisticated (and most useful) applications of agentic technology, it’s going to be the combination of local and cloud-based resources that offer the most effective responses to task-focused requests. Ultimately, success in this area will likely fall to whomever makes the process of combining these various datasets simpler - as well as offering the most intuitive tool to tap into these resources.

Another important benefit Amazon provides is that Quick builds on the company’s Bedrock platform and incorporates all the guardrails, access controls, security elements and other aspects of those tools. Finally, the new architecture that Quick takes advantage of provides even more local processing than tools like Claude Cowork do. While both tools leverage on-device silicon for file I/O, screen grabbing and compression, and certain autonomous tasks, Quick goes even further than Cowork in doing some of the actual file analysis locally.

Of course, the same capabilities that make desktop agents useful also make them potentially risky. Any tool that can read files, monitor applications, draft emails, trigger workflows and act across business systems needs extremely granular permissions, clear audit trails, human approval points and strong administrative controls. The winners in this market will not simply be the tools that can do the most; they will be the ones that enterprises trust to do the most safely.

As exciting as the movement towards client-based agentic AI workloads may be, it’s important to remember that most of the action in agentic AI is happening in cloud-focused places like Amazon Web Services. Still, it is great to see Amazon offering the potential for a more hybrid approach to these efforts, and hopefully, it’s a sign of similar developments to come.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

Disclosure: None.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Deutsche BöRse: Strong Execution Priced In, Valuation Caps Further Upside (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Following the Q1 2026 trading update, we are back to comment on Deutsche Börse AG (DBOEY)(DBOEF). As our readers will recall, we were previously constructive, then moved to a neutral stance, before reinstating a Buy rating in mid-February 2026. This proved to be a correct call. Given the solid stock price appreciation +26% (in this short time frame), and DB's valuation that reached our desired target price, we moved the company once again to an equal weight.

Our buy rating was supported by solid trading evidence (in the first 2026 months), higher forecasted earnings growth (with EPS growth from 7% to 8%), and the Allfunds transaction, structured with a lower cash outlay. Given the progressive DPS and the greater scope for buybacks (not lowered in the outstanding shares), we remained buyers.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Neutral Stance

DB's Q1 results were solid and illustrate the company's diversified business model. Geopolitical tensions and macroeconomic uncertainty, which can be headwinds for other industries, fueled higher earnings growth. In numbers, Deutsche Börse reported top-line sales up 12% (excluding treasury - Fig. 2), while operating costs increased by only 4%. This resulted in a massive +18% increase in EBITDA (and clearly demonstrated operating leverage). On a segment basis, results were supportive in Trading & Clearing, where net revenue surged by an impressive 14%. However, Fund Services and Securities Services were up 14% and 15%, respectively.

Given the strong Q1 results, Deutsche Börse reiterated its 2026 outlook.

Fig 2

Why are we neutral?

Our readers know that we built our investment thesis on European Stock Exchanges, given a volatile environment and the European appetite to invest in their home countries rather than channel capital into US securities. The former was reported in DB's headline called: "good start to 2026: structural growth delivers, and the model thrives amid volatility." That said, looking at Wall Street analysts' numbers, the company was only +1.9% above consensus expectations in top-line sales. There was a small miss in Trading & Clearing and the Investment Management Solutions. Q1 headline costs were €756 million (incl. D&A), +0.9% higher than consensus. This resulted in an EBITDA margin of 61.5%, 20 basis points below consensus expectations. Here at the Lab, this is minor, and we are not surprised to see a positive market reaction; our concern is related to Deutsche Börse's current valuation rather than its fundamentals.

In our last analysis, we have already increased our 2026 EPS growth expectation to 8% from 7%. In our last update, we did not account for the buyback uplift, which was calculated at 1%. As mentioned, after such a strong Q1, the CFO Jens Schulte commented, "With this good start to the year, we are fully on track to achieve our ambitious goals." Therefore, if market volatility moderates, the company's structural growth might be sufficient to meet these ambitious targets.

At the Capital Markets Day (Dec 2025), the company did not give a specific absolute EPS target, but it aimed for a 10% CAGR in cash EPS over 2026–2028. Even assuming a 10% EPS increase, this would result in an EPS of approximately €12.53, implying a target price of €250.60 per share (based on our 20x P/E target). Considering the current stock price of €267, we prefer to remain cautious. This EPS already accounts for the reduction in outstanding shares. This is evident from the fact that in our last update, the company disclosed outstanding shares for 188.3 million, and is now at 186.3 million

There are two considerations to add. We need to account for the strong start of the year in stock price performance. DB shares are nearly 30% up over the past 2.5 months. Secondly, and more importantly, the company significantly accelerated its buyback program (Fig. 3).

Fig 3

Valuation

We value companies from a fundamental view. This approach has always been positive in the long run. We know the company's shares might rise in the short term; however, we prefer to wait for a better entry point. Even assuming 10% EPS growth and a 20x P/E target, we do not reach a buy rating, with a price of €250.60 ($29.2 in ADR - 1 ADR = 1/10 of a German share). The company trades at a P/E of 21.4x and an EV/EBITDA of almost 16x. While this may be justified given an EBITDA margin above 60%, when we account for an FCF yield below 4%, we prefer to look at other targets. Our valuation is also aligned with the company's 5-year historical average (Fig. 4). In our last update, we did not consider the buyback uplift, and even if the company has not yet paid its 2026 dividend (ex-dividend date is on 14 May 2026), we are talking about a 1.6% yield (which is not sufficient to justify a buy rating). At the Lab, we believe there is a limited margin of error. If volatility moderates or EU investors maintain higher investment flows into the US, DB earnings might decelerate.

Fig 4

Risks

We have long covered Deutsche Börse, so we report the following risks: lower volatility, lower investment flows from the US to the EU (and lower investment among Europeans), and higher regulatory costs. The company might face greater competition from other major EU operators, such as LSEG and Euronext. Additionally, new players and technological updates are entering the market, intensifying the competitive environment (this includes Coinbase, which is launching perpetual-style derivatives, and Robinhood, which is exploring new derivative products). The company is valued at perfection (at least on a backward-looking basis), and there are integration risks given the Allfunds acquisition.

Conclusion

Deutsche Börse's "proven business model in volatile times" is delivering exceptional results, which are being translated into share price appreciation. From a valuation standpoint, we see limited support. For this reason, we prefer to remain neutral.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lenovo: Long-Term Gain
data.description:
Summary

Lenovo's (LNVGY) CEO indicated that the company will become an AI-native organization and is transforming its business model from legacy PCs to more AI related enterprise software and hardware solutions. Lenovo has been aggressive with recent acquisitions to help with this transformation and is slowly diversifying its operating income from being overly dependent on PCs. The stock is trading at a 58% discount to peers which is a good entry levels for longer-term investors.

Three distinct product groups:

Lenovo is a global technology company whose products are broken into three distinct categories: IDG, ISG and SSG. IDG (Intelligent Devices Group) provides consumer hardware such as PCs, smartphones, and tablets. ISG (Infrastructure Services Group) provides enterprise-level hardware and solutions such as AI servers, storage devices, cooling devices, infrastructure as a service solutions and edge computing. SSG (Solutions and Services Group) provides managed services and support, AI solutions, and XaaS services.

The SSG segment is the most profitable, with operating margins reaching 22.5% (+2.1% pts yoy) in CY4Q25. During the same time frame, SSG reached 34.3% of group operating profit (+2.8% yoy) while IDG's percentage of operating profit fell to 66.3% (-2.1% yoy) during the same time frame. IDG's operating margins were flattish on a yoy basis. ISG's operating margins fell by 0.2% pts yoy and its percentage of operating profit fell by 0.7% pts yoy during CY4Q25 due to restructuring costs incurred during the period.

Key drivers within IDG

Lenovo has the highest market share globally within PCs at 25% and during the last reported quarter, grew its market share by 1% pts and is now 5% pts ahead of the second largest PC manufacturer. The IDG segment remains healthy, with sales and operating profit growing by mid teens % yoy supported by higher priced products such as gaming and AI related PCs. Lenovo's smartphones via the Motorola brand also saw record volumes due to new model launches, strong monetization from app sharing revenue, and higher ASPs from more integrated AI features. Lenovo introduced its AI agent Qira this year which will personalize services across all of Lenovo's consumer platforms.

Key drivers within ISG

Lenovo aims to return this segment to profitability by the next reporting quarter. Inference AI and data center expansion, along with increased customer growth among enterprise, small-medium business, and cloud service providers, will be one of the primary drivers for this division. Lenovo's AI server business saw high double-digit year-over-year growth in CY4Q25, because of a strong product pipeline along with the introduction of the rack-scale data center based on the NVIDIA GB300 NVL72 platform. The GB300 Blackwell chip from Nvidia is the fastest AI processing chip currently available. Lenovo's liquid-cooling revenue also increased by 300% yoy.

Key drivers within SSG

Managed services and project solutions serving digital workplaces, hybrid clouds, sustainability, and industry specific solutions aimed at various end markets such as retail and sports are driving SSG segment growth. Managed services and project solutions have higher margins than the support service segment, as the products offered are more highly valued. As a percentage of revenue in the previous quarter, managed services + project solutions was 60% of revenue within SSG.

Why PCs may no longer matter

Leading technology market research firm Gartner has forecasted PC demand to fall by 10% yoy and smartphone demand to decline by 8% yoy in 2026. This is due to shortages in key components such as memory, which is expected to drive up these component costs (+130% yoy in 2026) and flow through to higher costs for the PC and smartphone end products. Hence, consumers are expected to delay product replacement cycles due to higher price points. Despite the weakness in PCs shipments, YTD, Lenovo's share price is +24% as I believe the stock is being valued more on its future growth potential from AI initiatives in enterprise hardware and software as opposed to being valued on its traditional PC segment.

$100bn in revenue in 2 years due to AI

Yang Yuanqing, Chairman and CEO of Lenovo Group in early April stated that Lenovo has an aim to achieve $100bn in revenue with a net profit margin of 5% in two years fueled by AI-related revenue. The goal is to transform Lenovo into an AI-native company where AI-related revenue doubled yoy in the previous quarter and is now about 1/3 of total group revenue. As a comparison base, for the fiscal year ending March 2025, Lenovo's revenue was $69bn with a net profit margin of 2%.

Recent company initiatives to transform into an AI-native company include several recent transactions. In early April, Lenovo completed its purchase of Infinidat which allows Lenovo to pivot into the higher end storage space. In late April, Lenovo announced the completion of Phoenix Technology acquisition. The acquisition brings firmware software development to Lenovo. Earlier in March, Lenovo announced Auto AI Box—an agentic auto AI platform that will allow for the vehicle to personalize driver preferences such as music and climate control settings. The platform is based on the Nvidia Drive AGX Thor system-on-a-chip to handle high-order AI processing.

Low valuation compared to sector

On every valuation metric, Lenovo is trading at a discount to the broader information technology sector and is rated as an A valuation grade on Seeking Alpha. For example, on a forward P/E multiple, the stock is trading at a 58% discount to the peer average on only 13.7x forward earnings (vs. sector average of 33x). Given that the lower margin PC segment is still 2/3 of group operating profit, I would apply a valuation discount to Lenovo vs. the peer average. Assuming a discount of 40% compared to the sector average would result in a target forward P/E multiple of around 20x or roughly 45% share price upside from current levels. I recommend a BUY on Lenovo's stock.

Risks

The key risk is enterprise hardware AI solutions are a competitive space, which may lead to margin compression. In addition, if Lenovo is not able to develop solutions in a faster or more cost effective manner compared to key competitors such as Dell and HP, Lenovo's AI solutions may not see significant market acceptance. Furthermore, if the PC segment slows at a faster than expected pace, near-term profitability would take a hit given that Lenovo still depends on this segment for 1/3 of its operating profit. Finally, investor sentiment on AI stocks can suddenly sour due to issues such as valuation concerns which would cause a share price correction, especially given the recent rally in Lenovo shares.

Conclusion

Lenovo is now positioning itself as a transformative AI-native company. The percentage of operating profit contributed from the PC segment is slowly declining due to greater contribution from the managed services and solutions segments, which utilize AI solutions to a greater extent. The stock is trading at a low valuation of 13x forward P/E which is a 58% discount to the peer average, which I believe is an attractive entry point for long term investors, where I expect a 45% share price upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
